military rifle scopes, Leatherwood Brothers
Utilize Accessories for Defense from Local Sources
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Action Steps:

1. Check Local Gun Shops and Army Surplus Outlets: Many accessories can be found locally at gun shops or Army surplus stores.
2. Contact Sherwood Distributors, Inc.: For additional options, contact Sherwood Distributors,...
Action Steps:

1. Check Local Gun Shops and Army Surplus Outlets: Many accessories can be found locally at gun shops or Army surplus stores.
2. Contact Sherwood Distributors, Inc.: For additional options, contact Sherwood Distributors, Inc., located at 18714 Parthenia Street, Northridge, California 91324. Request their catalog for available accessories.
3. Reach Out to Ruvel Company: Another option is the Ruvel Company, located at 3037 N. Clark Street, Chicago, Illinois 60657. Write to them for a catalog of available items.
accessories, defense equipment, local sources

Crowd Management (3) -

Controlling Social Unrest and Public Disorder
High Priority Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Develop a comprehensive plan for managing social unrest that includes communication, law enforcement coordination, and community engagement.
2. Deploy trained personnel to high-risk areas with clear instructions on de-escalation...
Procedure:

1. Develop a comprehensive plan for managing social unrest that includes communication, law enforcement coordination, and community engagement.
2. Deploy trained personnel to high-risk areas with clear instructions on de-escalation techniques and crowd control strategies.
3. Use non-lethal tools such as barriers, loudspeakers, and tear gas only when necessary and in accordance with legal guidelines.
4. Coordinate with local leaders and organizations to address the root causes of unrest and promote peaceful resolution.
5. Monitor public sentiment through social media and other platforms to anticipate and respond to potential outbreaks of disorder.
social unrest,crowd control,de-escalation techniques,community engagement,public order
Dealing with an Aggressive Crowd
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Remain calm and self-controlled. This may well have a calming effect on the situation. People will then be willing to help you. They can also tell you about security-related issues and about local needs and capabilities.
aggressive crowd, de-escalation, calm response
Handling Panic and Maintaining Public Order
High Priority Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Develop a public communication strategy to provide accurate information during an outbreak.
2. Deploy law enforcement and emergency management personnel to high-risk areas where panic is likely to occur.
3. Establish temporary...
Procedure:

1. Develop a public communication strategy to provide accurate information during an outbreak.
2. Deploy law enforcement and emergency management personnel to high-risk areas where panic is likely to occur.
3. Establish temporary shelters or distribution centers for affected individuals, ensuring they are well-staffed and equipped.
4. Use social media and traditional media platforms to disseminate calming messages and instructions.
5. Coordinate with community leaders and organizations to assist in managing public sentiment and preventing unrest.
public order,panic management,communication strategy,temporary shelters,crowd control

Uxo Hazard Response (3) -

Determine Appropriate Evacuation Distance for UXO Hazard
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Evacuate personnel and equipment from the area surrounding the UXO:

(1) For bombs, dispensers, large projected munitions (90-mm diameter and larger), evacuate a 360-degree perimeter at least 600 meters.

(2) For submunitions,...
Evacuate personnel and equipment from the area surrounding the UXO:

(1) For bombs, dispensers, large projected munitions (90-mm diameter and larger), evacuate a 360-degree perimeter at least 600 meters.

(2) For submunitions, placed, thrown, small projected munitions (smaller than 90-mm diameter), evacuate a 360-degree perimeter at least 300 meters.

If personnel or equipment cannot be evacuated, seek as much frontal and overhead cover as possible.

If UXO is suspected to have a chemical agent, ensure all personnel stay upwind of the item and are in full MOPP.
UXO, unexploded ordnance, evacuation distance, MOPP gear, chemical agent
Mark an Unexploded Ordnance (UXO) Hazard
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Mark the location (without approaching any closer) with some sort of recognizable material (such as white engineer tape, marking ribbon, clothing, or signs).

Place marker above ground at waist level if possible. Note physical terrain...
Mark the location (without approaching any closer) with some sort of recognizable material (such as white engineer tape, marking ribbon, clothing, or signs).

Place marker above ground at waist level if possible. Note physical terrain features of the location and route back to the UXO in order for the EOD team to return to dispose of the UXO.
UXO, unexploded ordnance, marking hazard, EOD team, terrain features
Report an Unexploded Ordnance (UXO) Hazard Using the 9-Line Explosive Hazard Spot Report
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Report UXO hazard to your chain of command. You or your chain of command should request explosive ordnance disposal (EOD) support using the 9-line Explosive Hazard Spot Report:

(1) Date and time of the fall, impact, or finding of the...
Report UXO hazard to your chain of command. You or your chain of command should request explosive ordnance disposal (EOD) support using the 9-line Explosive Hazard Spot Report:

(1) Date and time of the fall, impact, or finding of the UXO item(s).

(2) The exact location of item(s), including grid coordinate (8-digit minimum), landmarks, reference points, or street addresses.

(3) The name and organization of person reporting the incident, including call sign or phone number.

(4) Identify UXO by type (dropped, projected, thrown, placed) and subgroup.

(5) Any CBRN contamination present?

(6) What resources are threatened?

(7) How the UXO has affected unit mission.

(8) The safety measures that have been taken including the evacuation distances that have been accomplished.

(9) The requested priority for receiving EOD support (immediate, indirect, minor, no threat).

Be prepared to provide a guide for the EOD team.
UXO, unexploded ordnance, EOD support, 9-line report, CBRN contamination

Combat Survival (3) -

Check Barbed Wire for Booby Traps or Early Warning Devices
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Look for booby traps or early warning devices attached to the barbed wire.
2. Throw a grappling hook with a length of rope attached over the barbed wire.
3. **Pull the rope to set off any booby traps or early warning...
Procedure:

1. Look for booby traps or early warning devices attached to the barbed wire.
2. Throw a grappling hook with a length of rope attached over the barbed wire.
3. Pull the rope to set off any booby traps or early warning devices.
barbed wire,booby trap,early warning device,grappling hook
Cross Exposed Danger Areas
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Select a point at or near a bend in the road or stream. If possible, select a bend that has cover and concealment on both sides.
2. Crawl up to the edge of the open area.
3. **Observe the other side carefully before...
Procedure:

1. Select a point at or near a bend in the road or stream. If possible, select a bend that has cover and concealment on both sides.
2. Crawl up to the edge of the open area.
3. Observe the other side carefully before crossing.
danger area,crossing,cover,concealment
Cross Over Barbed Wire Using Protective Materials
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Throw the wood, mat, or chicken wire over the barbed wire.
2. Cross carefully because such a mat or net forms an unstable path.
barbed wire,wood,chicken wire,mat,crossing

Training Procedures (3) -

Conducting a Commercial Vehicle Search Evaluation Setup
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Conducting a Commercial Vehicle Search Evaluation Setup

Steps:

1. Provide the Soldier with a commercial vehicle to search in a well-lit area.
2. **Use vehicles in the DOD inventory if civilian commercial vehicles are not...
Procedure for Conducting a Commercial Vehicle Search Evaluation Setup

Steps:

1. Provide the Soldier with a commercial vehicle to search in a well-lit area.
2. Use vehicles in the DOD inventory if civilian commercial vehicles are not available.
3. Identify a role player driver and a role player occupant for the commercial vehicle.
4. Ensure that the role players are authorized to operate the equipment prior to beginning this task.
5. Notify the role players that they are to comply with and follow all instructions given by the Soldier searching the vehicle.
6. Have the role players place a dummy explosive device or dummy prohibited item somewhere in, under, or on the commercial vehicle to be searched prior to the Soldiers arriving to test.
7. The dummy explosive device or dummy prohibited item can be handmade. It should not be large enough to gain immediate discovery yet not so small that it cannot be found by a highly motivated Soldier conducting a reasonable search.
8. Give the Soldier a search mirror, a flashlight, a reflective vest, and the local SOP.
vehicle inspection training, role players, dummy explosive device, evaluation setup
Performance Measures for Commercial Vehicle Search Task
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Performance Measures in a Commercial Vehicle Search Task

Steps:

1. Stopped the vehicle using hand-and-arm signals.
2. Directed the driver to turn off the engine and apply the parking brake.
3. **Interviewed the driver...
Procedure for Performance Measures in a Commercial Vehicle Search Task

Steps:

1. Stopped the vehicle using hand-and-arm signals.
2. Directed the driver to turn off the engine and apply the parking brake.
3. Interviewed the driver and vehicle occupants.
4. Directed the occupants to exit the vehicle.
5. Identified the type of commercial vehicle being searched.
6. Searched the undercarriage and chassis of the vehicle.
7. Searched the identified commercial vehicle, paying particular attention to high-threat 'hot spot' areas.
8. Took action when the search revealed suspected explosive devices or prohibited items in the vehicle.
9. Released the driver and occupants if no explosive devices or prohibited items were found or if suspected items were cleared by military police or EOD personnel.
vehicle inspection training, performance measures, hot spot areas
Prepare for Access Control Evaluation Task
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Preparing for an Access Control Evaluation Task:

1. Provide a suitable training site that is well-lit and appropriate for access control operations.
2. Use DOD inventory vehicles with a DOD decal, if available, or use...
Procedure for Preparing for an Access Control Evaluation Task:

1. Provide a suitable training site that is well-lit and appropriate for access control operations.
2. Use DOD inventory vehicles with a DOD decal, if available, or use civilian vehicles with a DOD decal.
3. Identify role players to act as either drivers or pedestrians attempting entry onto the installation.
4. Ensure that role players are authorized to operate equipment before beginning the task.
5. Instruct role players to comply with instructions given by the Soldier being tested.
6. The tester will determine what type of ID card the role players use.

Notes:
• This setup is critical for ensuring that the evaluation accurately reflects real-world access control scenarios.
• Ensure all safety protocols are followed during training exercises.
access control training, evaluation setup, role players, security simulation

Inspections (3) -

Conduct Periodic Spot Checks of Personnel
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

• Conduct periodic spot checks of personnel to ensure compliance with security protocols.

Details:
• Randomly inspect personnel for unauthorized items or materials.
• Ensure that all individuals are in compliance with special...
Procedure:

• Conduct periodic spot checks of personnel to ensure compliance with security protocols.

Details:
• Randomly inspect personnel for unauthorized items or materials.
• Ensure that all individuals are in compliance with special orders and security requirements.
spot checks,personnel,security protocols,special orders
Search Packages and Vehicles Before Entry or Exit
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

• Search all packages before allowing them to enter or exit the restricted area.
• Search vehicles before allowing them to enter or exit the restricted area.

Details:
• Conduct thorough inspections of all items, including...
Procedure:

• Search all packages before allowing them to enter or exit the restricted area.
• Search vehicles before allowing them to enter or exit the restricted area.

Details:
• Conduct thorough inspections of all items, including packages and vehicles, for prohibited materials or contraband.
• Ensure that no harmful or unauthorized items are brought into or removed from the restricted area.
package search,vehicle search,restricted area,contraband
Identify Inspection Responsibilities
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Procedure:

1. Determine who is responsible for inspections:
- Assign specific personnel or teams to conduct regular system and facility inspections.
2. Define inspection frequency:
- Establish a schedule for routine checks based...
Procedure:

1. Determine who is responsible for inspections:
- Assign specific personnel or teams to conduct regular system and facility inspections.
2. Define inspection frequency:
- Establish a schedule for routine checks based on the criticality of the system or area being inspected.
3. Document inspection procedures:
- Create detailed instructions for conducting inspections, including checklists and reporting requirements.
4. Ensure accountability:
- Maintain records of all inspections to ensure compliance with standards and regulations.
inspection responsibilities,frequency,documentation,accountability

Detainee Search And Restraint (3) -

Conducting a Prone Frisk Search of a Detainee
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Use the bending and crushing technique to remove items as they are discovered and set them aside.
2. Bend the seams to determine if razor blades or similar devices are hidden.
3. **Grasp loose clothing, pull it away from...
Procedure:

1. Use the bending and crushing technique to remove items as they are discovered and set them aside.
2. Bend the seams to determine if razor blades or similar devices are hidden.
3. Grasp loose clothing, pull it away from the skin, and squeeze it to detect objects hidden under or within clothing.
4. Cover each area by repeating the crushing technique until you are sure there are no hidden objects.
5. If you find a weapon at any point during the search:
- Loudly announce the weapon found so that the guard and interpreter can clearly hear (e.g., gun, knife, or razor).
- Alert the guard.
- Remain in firm contact with the detainee as you remove the weapon from its hiding place.
- Stand up with the weapon, being careful not to walk between the guard and the detainee, and place the weapon a safe distance away within view of the guard.
- Return to the detainee and continue searching.
prone frisk search, weapon detection, contraband search, security protocol
Directing a Detainee into a Search Position
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Stay out of the reach of the detainee.
2. Direct the detainee to stand and face you.
- If an interpreter is not available, demonstrate the desired movement to overcome the language barrier.
3. **Direct him/her to raise...
Procedure:

1. Stay out of the reach of the detainee.
2. Direct the detainee to stand and face you.
- If an interpreter is not available, demonstrate the desired movement to overcome the language barrier.
3. Direct him/her to raise his/her arms above the head, lock elbows, and spread his/hers fingers with the palms facing you.
4. Visually check the detainee's hands for weapons or contraband.
5. Order the detainee to turn around and drop to his/her knees.
6. Search the back of the detainee's hands for weapons or contraband.
7. Direct the detainee to lie on his/her stomach with arms straight out to the sides with the palms up, and forehead on the ground.
8. Tell the detainee to spread his/her legs as far apart as possible with the feet outward and the heels in contact with the ground.
9. Ensure that the guard remains in front of and at an oblique angle to the detainee (opposite the side being searched).
detainee search, restraint procedure, security protocol, hands check, position for search
Handling of Weapons Found During a Detainee Search
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. If you find a weapon at any point during the search, loudly announce the weapon found so that the guard and interpreter can clearly hear (e.g., gun, knife, or razor).
2. Alert the guard immediately.
3. **Remain in firm...
Procedure:

1. If you find a weapon at any point during the search, loudly announce the weapon found so that the guard and interpreter can clearly hear (e.g., gun, knife, or razor).
2. Alert the guard immediately.
3. Remain in firm contact with the detainee as you remove the weapon from its hiding place.
4. Stand up with the weapon, being careful not to walk between the guard and the detainee, and place the weapon a safe distance away within view of the guard.
5. Return to the detainee and continue searching.
weapon handling, detainee search protocol, security procedure

Detainee Transportation (3) -

Prepare for Actions at Stops and Maintain Contact with Higher Headquarters
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

b. Prepare for actions at the planned stops and anticipate actions at unexpected stops.

c. Maintain continuous contact with higher headquarters and execute the mission according to the local standing operating procedure...
Procedure:

b. Prepare for actions at the planned stops and anticipate actions at unexpected stops.

c. Maintain continuous contact with higher headquarters and execute the mission according to the local standing operating procedure (SOP).

• d. Direct response actions during emergencies.

• e. Correct, report, and document any allegations of detainee abuse.
stops, prepare, emergency response, higher headquarters, SOP
Provide Information and Transfer Custody of Detainees
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

• d. Provide any additional information regarding the circumstances of capture.

• e. Provide a unit point of contact for any necessary follow-up.

f. Have the escort supervisor sign the release of confiscated items on...
Procedure:

• d. Provide any additional information regarding the circumstances of capture.

• e. Provide a unit point of contact for any necessary follow-up.

f. Have the escort supervisor sign the release of confiscated items on the DA Form 4137 when the joint inventory is completed. The gaining authority signs the receipt of confiscated items on the DA Form 4137, prepares and signs the DD Form 2708,and returns the completed receipt to the escort supervisor.

g. Transfer custody of the detainee, respective documents, and confiscated items.

h. Brief facility personnel on all issues or information pertaining to the detainees or their conduct during the escort.
custody transfer, DD Form 2708, DA Form 4137, brief facility personnel
Review Detainee Escort Manifest with Facility Personnel
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

b. Review the detainee escort manifest with assigned facility personnel.

c. Conduct a joint inventory of all detainees, pertinent documents, and confiscated items using the escort manifest, to include—

- (1) DD Form...
Procedure:

b. Review the detainee escort manifest with assigned facility personnel.

c. Conduct a joint inventory of all detainees, pertinent documents, and confiscated items using the escort manifest, to include—

- (1) DD Form 2745 (Parts A, B, and C).

- (2) DA Form 4137.

- (3) Medical files or information if available.
manifest, inventory, DD Form 2745, DA Form 4137, facility personnel

Community Safety (3) -

Form a Neighborhood Watch for Post-Disaster Security
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Action: Forming a neighborhood watch is recommended as an alternative to self-appointed militias or vigilantism.

Important Note: Self-protection may be necessary, but forming organized groups like militias is discouraged.
neighborhood watch, post-disaster security
Be Alert to Suspicious Activity
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Be Alert to Suspicious Activity:

1. Stay vigilant and observe your surroundings for any unusual or suspicious behavior.
2. Report any suspicious activity (e.g., unattended packages, loitering individuals) to local law enforcement or emergency...
Be Alert to Suspicious Activity:

1. Stay vigilant and observe your surroundings for any unusual or suspicious behavior.
2. Report any suspicious activity (e.g., unattended packages, loitering individuals) to local law enforcement or emergency management officials immediately.
3. Encourage neighbors and community members to do the same and share information about potential threats.
4. Stay informed about current threat levels by checking cable news networks or visiting [www.dhs.gov](https://www.dhs.gov).
5. Keep your family informed of any changes in threat levels and review the appropriate actions for each level.
suspicious,activity,safety,threat,reporting
Participate in Neighborhood Watch and Volunteers in Police Service (VIPS)
Standard Source: FEMA Citizen Preparedness Guide
Participate in Neighborhood Watch and Volunteers in Police Service (VIPS)

• Join Neighborhood Watch (nnw.org) or Volunteers in Police Service (theiacp.org/VIPS).
• These programs empower the public to protect their community through volunteer...
Participate in Neighborhood Watch and Volunteers in Police Service (VIPS)

• Join Neighborhood Watch (nnw.org) or Volunteers in Police Service (theiacp.org/VIPS).
• These programs empower the public to protect their community through volunteer crime prevention.
• Participate in neighborhood patrols, safety meetings, and other initiatives.
neighborhood watch,vips,civil defense,crime prevention

Crime Prevention (3) -

Be Aware of Increased Crime During Evacuation and Displacement
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Procedure:

1. Expect an increase in crime (e.g., theft, assault, vandalism) when evacuees are housed in local communities.
2. Report any criminal activity to local law enforcement, even if they may downplay or deny it publicly.
3....
Procedure:

1. Expect an increase in crime (e.g., theft, assault, vandalism) when evacuees are housed in local communities.
2. Report any criminal activity to local law enforcement, even if they may downplay or deny it publicly.
3. Stay informed through trusted contacts within law enforcement about the true nature of crime levels in your area.
4. If you are a local resident, take additional security measures (e.g., increased surveillance, neighborhood watch) to protect yourself and property.
5. Be aware that authorities may not report or acknowledge rising crime rates, so it is important to rely on personal observation and trusted sources.

Warning: Increased crime may be underreported by officials, so vigilance and proactive measures are essential.
increased crime, evacuation, security, preparedness
Responding to Violent Crime in Public Spaces
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Stay Informed: Monitor local news, social media, and official alerts for updates on violent crimes such as robberies or hostage situations.
2. Avoid High-Risk Areas: If a bank, jewelry store, or other high-value...
Procedure:

1. Stay Informed: Monitor local news, social media, and official alerts for updates on violent crimes such as robberies or hostage situations.
2. Avoid High-Risk Areas: If a bank, jewelry store, or other high-value establishment is robbed, avoid the area unless absolutely necessary.
3. Follow Evacuation Orders: If buildings are evacuated or sealed off during negotiations with criminals, comply immediately and stay away from the location.
4. Do Not Engage: Avoid confronting suspects or attempting to intervene in active situations such as hostage-taking or armed robberies.
5. Secure Yourself: If you are inside a building that is being evacuated or sealed off, remain calm and follow instructions from law enforcement or emergency personnel.
6. Report Suspicious Activity: If you witness any suspicious behavior or activity related to violent crime, contact local authorities immediately.
7. Prepare for Emergencies: Keep an emergency kit with essentials such as water, food, flashlight, and first-aid supplies in case of prolonged evacuations.
violent crime,response,emergency preparedness,evacuation,hostage,safety
Managing a Crime Wave in the Community
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Stay Alert: Be aware of any unusual patterns or increases in minor crimes such as theft, vandalism, or harassment.
2. Report Crimes Immediately: Notify local law enforcement about any suspicious activity or criminal...
Procedure:

1. Stay Alert: Be aware of any unusual patterns or increases in minor crimes such as theft, vandalism, or harassment.
2. Report Crimes Immediately: Notify local law enforcement about any suspicious activity or criminal behavior to help prevent escalation.
3. Secure Your Property: Lock doors and windows, install security systems, and avoid leaving valuables unattended.
4. Avoid High-Risk Areas: Limit travel to areas known for increased crime rates, especially during nighttime hours.
5. Community Vigilance: Participate in neighborhood watch programs or organize community patrols if local law enforcement is overwhelmed.
6. Arm Yourself Responsibly: If you choose to carry a weapon for self-defense, ensure it is legal and that you are trained in its use.
7. Stay Informed: Monitor news and social media for updates on crime trends and police activity in your area.
8. Support Local Law Enforcement: Provide information or assistance if needed during investigations or community safety efforts.
crime wave,community safety,self defense,neighborhood watch,emergency preparedness

Firearms Safety (3) -

Understanding the Cardinal Rule of Gun Ownership
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Finally, there is a cardinal rule about guns that should always be kept in mind: A gun in your hand is totally worthless against an assailant unless you’re fully willing to use it to defend yourself.

This means that simply owning a weapon...
Finally, there is a cardinal rule about guns that should always be kept in mind: A gun in your hand is totally worthless against an assailant unless you’re fully willing to use it to defend yourself.

This means that simply owning a weapon is not enough; you must also be mentally prepared and ready to act decisively when the time comes.

If you are ever faced with a life-threatening situation, do not hesitate to use your gun for self-defense. Remember: you must stop an assailant, not just wound him. A minor injury may not prevent them from continuing their attack, so it’s important to be prepared to fire until the threat is neutralized.
gun ownership rules, self-defense, firearm use, emergency readiness
Gun Safety and Training for Emergency Situations
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
If you know someone who has a good bit of knowledge and experience with guns, get him to teach you how to shoot safely. It is a terrible mistake to have a gun and not know anything about proper shooting and gun safety.

Look for local **gun...
If you know someone who has a good bit of knowledge and experience with guns, get him to teach you how to shoot safely. It is a terrible mistake to have a gun and not know anything about proper shooting and gun safety.

Look for local gun courses in your area and take them if available. Ask about these at your local gun shop or firearms dealer.

It’s also important to keep your guns away from your children! Store them where you can get to them quickly if needed, but ensure that they are inaccessible to children. There are lockable gun boxes on the market that are quick for adults to access but impossible for a child to open.
gun safety, firearm training, emergency preparedness, children and guns
Implement Firearms Safety Training
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Objective: Ensure all group members understand firearms safety to prevent accidents.

Steps:
1. Assign a qualified individual or team to lead firearms safety training sessions.
2. Teach the fundamentals of gun safety, including proper...
Objective: Ensure all group members understand firearms safety to prevent accidents.

Steps:
1. Assign a qualified individual or team to lead firearms safety training sessions.
2. Teach the fundamentals of gun safety, including proper handling and storage of weapons.
3. Emphasize the importance of keeping loaded firearms away from children and pacifists.
4. Conduct drills to ensure all members are proficient in basic firearm safety procedures.
5. Ensure that everyone understands the risks associated with improper use of firearms.
firearms training, gun safety, emergency preparedness

Terrorism Response (3) -

Respond to a Bomb Threat
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Objective: Respond appropriately when receiving or being notified of a bomb threat.

Procedure:
1. If you receive a bomb threat, stay calm and get as much information from the caller as possible, including details about the location and...
Objective: Respond appropriately when receiving or being notified of a bomb threat.

Procedure:
1. If you receive a bomb threat, stay calm and get as much information from the caller as possible, including details about the location and nature of the threat.
2. Keep the caller on the line and record everything said for law enforcement.
3. Immediately notify local police and building management.
4. Do not touch any suspicious packages or objects that may be involved in the threat.
5. Clear the area around any suspicious package and ensure it is visible to emergency responders.
6. During an evacuation, avoid standing near windows or other potentially hazardous areas.
7. Do not block sidewalks or streets for emergency vehicles; move out of the way immediately.
bomb threat, emergency response, evacuation, security, police notification
Responding to an Explosive Device Detonation in Public Spaces
Critical Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure for Responding to an Explosive Device Detonation in Public Spaces:

1. Immediate Evacuation and Alerting:
- If you are near the detonation, immediately move away from the blast area to a safe location.
- Alert others nearby...
Procedure for Responding to an Explosive Device Detonation in Public Spaces:

1. Immediate Evacuation and Alerting:
- If you are near the detonation, immediately move away from the blast area to a safe location.
- Alert others nearby by shouting or using a whistle if available.
- Call emergency services (999) as soon as possible.

2. Assess for Injuries and Provide First Aid:
- Check yourself and others for injuries, prioritizing those who are unconscious or not breathing.
- Administer basic first aid such as stopping bleeding, immobilizing suspected fractures, and ensuring airway clearance.
- Do not move seriously injured individuals unless there is an immediate danger (e.g., fire or further explosions).

3. Avoid the Blast Area:
- Stay clear of the blast site to avoid secondary explosions or structural collapse.
- Follow instructions from emergency services and local authorities regarding evacuation routes and assembly points.

4. Provide Information to Emergency Services:
- When contacting emergency services, provide your location, details about the explosion (e.g., time, number of people injured), and any visible hazards such as fire or leaking materials.

5. Support for Casualties and Displaced Individuals:
- If you are in a position to assist, help direct casualties to shelter or first aid stations if available.
- Be prepared for long-term support from local, regional, and national victim support structures, including psychological assistance and temporary housing.

6. Be Aware of Potential Aftermath Effects:
- Explosions may cause fires, structural collapse, and damage to nearby buildings or infrastructure (e.g., utility lines).
- Be prepared for delays in hospital services and potential long-term impacts on local businesses and tourism.

7. Cooperation with Emergency Response Teams:
- Allow access for emergency responders such as Explosive Ordnance Disposal teams, Urban Search and Rescue units, and forensic experts.
- Follow any instructions from these teams regarding restricted areas or safety protocols.

8. Long-Term Recovery Considerations:
- Be aware that recovery may take weeks or months, depending on the scale of damage.
- Support local authorities in rebuilding efforts and be prepared for temporary displacement if your residence is affected.
explosive device, terrorist attack, emergency response, evacuation, first aid, structural collapse
Responding to a Marauding Terrorist Attack in Public Spaces
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Immediate Action by Bystanders and Venue Staff:
- If you are in a public space or venue and witness an active attack, prioritize your safety and the safety of others.
- Call 999 (emergency services) immediately if it is...
Procedure:

1. Immediate Action by Bystanders and Venue Staff:
- If you are in a public space or venue and witness an active attack, prioritize your safety and the safety of others.
- Call 999 (emergency services) immediately if it is safe to do so.
- Follow instructions from emergency responders or venue staff.

2. Evacuation Procedures:
- If evacuation is necessary, follow clearly marked exit routes and avoid using elevators.
- Assist individuals with disabilities or those who may need help evacuating.
- Do not re-enter the affected area until it has been declared safe by authorities.

3. Use of Emergency Services:
- Ensure that emergency services have access to the location, and provide accurate information about the nature of the attack (e.g., use of firearms, knives, or vehicles).
- If possible, guide responders to the nearest safe entry point.

4. Support for Casualties:
- Provide first aid if trained and it is safe to do so.
- Do not attempt to move injured individuals unless there is an immediate danger (e.g., fire or further attack).
- Wait for professional medical assistance to arrive.

5. Post-Attack Actions:
- Cooperate with law enforcement during investigations and provide any relevant information.
- Follow official guidance on returning to the affected area, which may include temporary restrictions or security checks.
- Be prepared for potential disruptions in local services such as transport, healthcare, and business operations.
terrorist attack,response procedure,public space,evacuation,emergency services,casualty support

Personal Protection (2) -

Prepare Security Supplies Kit
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Purpose: Ensure safety and protection during emergencies.

Items to Include:
1. Common Caliber Ammunition: Top choices include .22 long rifle, .223 Remington (5.56 mm NATO), .308 Winchester (7.62 mm NATO), .3006, 12 gauge (2-3/4 inch #4...
Purpose: Ensure safety and protection during emergencies.

Items to Include:
1. Common Caliber Ammunition: Top choices include .22 long rifle, .223 Remington (5.56 mm NATO), .308 Winchester (7.62 mm NATO), .3006, 12 gauge (2-3/4 inch #4 Buckshot), .45 ACP, and 9mm Parabellum.

Quantities to Include:
1. .22 shells: 1000 units (purchase by 7-1-08, 90-day duration)
2. Gun safe: 1 unit (purchase by 4-1-08, 30-day duration)
3. Guns/Ammo: Assortment of firearms and ammunition (purchase by 4-1-08, 30-day duration)
4. Military rifle bore cleaner: 10 bottles of 1 oz each (purchase by 7-1-08, 90-day duration)
5. Ammo reloader: 1 unit (purchase by 10-1-08, 1-year duration)
6. Ammo Cans: 5 units (purchase by 4-1-08, 30-day duration)
7. Gun accessories: 1 set per weapon (purchase by 4-1-08, 30-day duration)
8. Gun cleaning equipment: 1 set per weapon (purchase by 4-1-08, 30-day duration)
9. Military web gear: 2 units per person (purchase by 4-1-08, 30-day duration) - includes pistol belts, magazine pouches, etc.
10. Perimeter alarm of some sort: 1 set (purchase by 4-1-08, 30-day duration)
11. Solar powered perimeter Lights: 5 units (purchase by 4-1-08, 30-day duration)
12. Waterproof dufflebags (“dry bags”): 1 per person (purchase by 4-1-08, 30-day duration)

Notes:
• Common caliber ammunition is considered the best all-around barter item.
• Military web gear includes items like pistol belts and magazine pouches that may suddenly be needed.
security supplies, firearms, ammunition, protection, emergency kit, preparedness
Use of Personal Passive-Protection Equipment in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• If you come to count on passive-protection equipment to do your job, then you should probably not be doing it.
• This includes items such as:
- A flak jacket;
- A ballistic (bullet-proof) jacket;
- A safety helmet, which must...
Procedure:

• If you come to count on passive-protection equipment to do your job, then you should probably not be doing it.
• This includes items such as:
- A flak jacket;
- A ballistic (bullet-proof) jacket;
- A safety helmet, which must always be worn together with a protective jacket covering the chest, back and neck.
• Instructions for use are given with the equipment. Generally speaking, if you have personal passive-protection gear:
- Have it with you just in case you find yourself in a particularly dangerous situation;
- Be aware that it always increases the risk of you being mistaken for a soldier, policeman, a member of an armed group, etc.
- Do not feel you are immune and fully protected.
- Do not use it when it is not needed.
passive protection, flak jacket, ballistic gear, safety helmet

Quarantine Enforcement (2) -

Develop Protocols for Monitoring and Enforcing Quarantine Measures
High Priority Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Develop protocols for monitoring and enforcing quarantine measures, such as:

1. **Protocols for follow-up of persons who cannot be reached by telephone. These may include a threshold period for nonresponsiveness that should trigger a home visit...
Develop protocols for monitoring and enforcing quarantine measures, such as:

1. Protocols for follow-up of persons who cannot be reached by telephone. These may include a threshold period for nonresponsiveness that should trigger a home visit or other means to locate the person. Partnerships with law enforcement and other community-based resources will be helpful in tracing the whereabouts of persons who have violated restrictions.

2. Protocols for monitoring persons who cannot or will not comply with voluntary home quarantine:
- Issuing official, legally binding quarantine orders
- Posting a guard outside the home
- Using electronic forms of monitoring
- Using guarded facilities

3. Protocols for using checkpoints to restrict travel between neighborhoods
quarantine enforcement, law enforcement, checkpoints, electronic monitoring, compliance protocols
Enforcing Quarantine Measures Effectively
High Priority Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Develop clear quarantine protocols that include guidelines for isolation periods and conditions.
2. Train personnel on how to enforce quarantine measures while ensuring the safety of both officers and individuals in quarantine.
3....
Procedure:

1. Develop clear quarantine protocols that include guidelines for isolation periods and conditions.
2. Train personnel on how to enforce quarantine measures while ensuring the safety of both officers and individuals in quarantine.
3. Use technology tools such as GPS tracking or mobile apps to monitor compliance with quarantine orders.
4. Provide support services, including food delivery and medical care, to individuals under quarantine to reduce non-compliance.
5. Coordinate with local authorities and healthcare providers to ensure that enforcement is consistent and humane.
quarantine protocols,enforcement,technology tools,support services,compliance monitoring

Biological Detection (2) -

Deploy and Operate the M31E1 Biological Integrated Detection System (BIDS)
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. System Assembly: Mount the S-788 lightweight multipurpose shelter on a MI097 Heavy High-Mobility Multipurpose Wheeled Vehicle.
2. Power Supply: Connect the PU-801 15-kw generator to provide electrical power for the...
Procedure:

1. System Assembly: Mount the S-788 lightweight multipurpose shelter on a MI097 Heavy High-Mobility Multipurpose Wheeled Vehicle.
2. Power Supply: Connect the PU-801 15-kw generator to provide electrical power for the system.
3. Detection Suite Activation:
- Activate aerodynamic particle sizing technology to analyze airborne particles.
- Turn on bioluminescence detection to identify biological agents.
- Use flow cytometry for rapid cell analysis.
- Employ mass spectrometry and immunoassay technologies in a complementary, layered manner to increase detection confidence.
4. Strategic Deployment: Deploy individual BIDS systems throughout the Corps area to create a sensor array/network for warning and confirming biological attacks.
5. Presumptive Identification: Use the system to presumptively identify the biological agent being used during an attack.
6. Sample Collection: Safely configure samples for later laboratory analysis.
7. Transportability: Ensure that the M31E1 BIDS is C-130 aircraft transportable, has roll-on/roll-off capability, and can operate in a dismounted role separate from its dedicated vehicle.
BIDS,biological detection,aerodynamic particle sizing,bioluminescence,flow cytometry,mass spectrometry,immunoassay
Operate the IBADS System for Biological and Toxicological Warfare Agent Detection
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. System Setup: Deploy the IBADS point detector system on a ship or in a designated location.
2. Component Activation:
- Turn on the particle size counter to analyze airborne particles.
- Activate the particle wet...
Procedure:

1. System Setup: Deploy the IBADS point detector system on a ship or in a designated location.
2. Component Activation:
- Turn on the particle size counter to analyze airborne particles.
- Activate the particle wet cyclone sampler to collect and process samples.
3. Agent Detection: Use the manual identifier (improved membrane calorimetric ticket flow-through assay) with antibody antigen tickets for BW agent identification in the ship's medical bay.
4. Warning Capability: The IBADS will detect and warn of the presence of 5 biological and toxicological warfare agents.
5. Deployment Readiness: Ensure that the Navy has rapid prototypes (25 total) ready for deployment with the fleet on short notice.
6. Technology Application: Use the same technology in the development of biological detectors for ports and airfields.
IBADS,biological warfare agent detection,particle size counter,wet cyclone sampler,antibody antigen tickets

Mine Placement (2) -

Proper Installation of Tripwires in Snow
High Priority Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure for Proper Installation of Tripwires in Snow

1. Place tripwires at various levels above the snow: This is especially important when using pull-action igniters.
2. Avoid placing tripwires beneath the snow surface: Tripwires...
Procedure for Proper Installation of Tripwires in Snow

1. Place tripwires at various levels above the snow: This is especially important when using pull-action igniters.
2. Avoid placing tripwires beneath the snow surface: Tripwires placed under the snow may freeze in and fail to function properly.
3. Paint tripwires white if time permits: This helps to make them less visible, reducing the likelihood of detection by enemy personnel.
4. Ensure proper tension and positioning: Tripwires should be taut and positioned so that they are triggered easily by movement.
tripwire installation, cold weather, pull-action igniters, mine security
Proper Placement of Antipersonnel Mines in Snow
High Priority Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure for Proper Placement of Antipersonnel Mines in Snow

1. Use pressure-type igniters: Ensure that the mine is placed approximately 3 cm (1") beneath the snow surface.
- This depth prevents the snow from acting as a “bridge” and...
Procedure for Proper Placement of Antipersonnel Mines in Snow

1. Use pressure-type igniters: Ensure that the mine is placed approximately 3 cm (1") beneath the snow surface.
- This depth prevents the snow from acting as a “bridge” and potentially preventing detonation.
2. Avoid burying too deeply: Mines buried too deeply in snow may not detonate due to the insulating effect of the snow.
3. Ensure weight distribution: When using pressure-type igniters, place the mine so that the maximum weight of an individual will be brought to bear on it.
4. Avoid bridging by equipment: Ensure that the mine is not positioned in a way that could be “bridged” by a ski or snowshoe, which may prevent detonation.
5. Consider terrain and trails: Mines can be placed on ski or snowshoe trails for maximum effectiveness.
antipersonnel mines, mine placement, cold weather, pressure-type igniters, snowshoe trails

Military Conduct (2) -

Discuss Relationships That Do Not Normally Constitute Fraternization
Reference Source: MA 1.02 USMC Fundamentals of Martial Arts
Objective: Discuss relationships between Marines that do not normally constitute fraternization in accordance with references.

Procedure:
1. Identify examples of acceptable relationships such as friendships, professional associations, and...
Objective: Discuss relationships between Marines that do not normally constitute fraternization in accordance with references.

Procedure:
1. Identify examples of acceptable relationships such as friendships, professional associations, and casual interactions that do not involve a superior-subordinate dynamic or compromise military effectiveness.
2. Emphasize the importance of distinguishing between appropriate and inappropriate relationships to avoid policy violations.
3. Ensure the discussion includes scenarios where personal relationships are maintained without affecting duty performance or unit cohesion.

Notes: This topic is critical for understanding military conduct and maintaining discipline within the Marine Corps.
non-fraternization relationships, military conduct, acceptable interactions
Discuss the Definition of Fraternization
Reference Source: MA 1.02 USMC Fundamentals of Martial Arts
Objective: Discuss the definition of fraternization in accordance with references.

Procedure:
1. Define fraternization as a relationship between Marines that is not permitted under Marine Corps policy, typically involving a...
Objective: Discuss the definition of fraternization in accordance with references.

Procedure:
1. Define fraternization as a relationship between Marines that is not permitted under Marine Corps policy, typically involving a superior-subordinate relationship or relationships that could compromise military effectiveness or discipline.
2. Emphasize that fraternization undermines unit cohesion and can lead to disciplinary action.
3. Ensure the discussion includes examples of inappropriate relationships such as romantic or personal relationships between Marines in positions of authority and those under their command.

Notes: This topic is critical for understanding military conduct and maintaining discipline within the Marine Corps.
fraternization, military policy, conduct, relationships

Guard Duty (2) -

Perform Duty as a Guard
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Objective: Perform guard duties in accordance with general orders and local SOPs.

Steps:
1. Report for duty at the assigned guard post, equipped with all necessary post-specific equipment.
2. **Follow general orders, special orders, and...
Objective: Perform guard duties in accordance with general orders and local SOPs.

Steps:
1. Report for duty at the assigned guard post, equipped with all necessary post-specific equipment.
2. Follow general orders, special orders, and local standing operating procedures (SOPs) to ensure compliance with security protocols.
3. Maintain constant vigilance over the area of responsibility, monitoring for any suspicious activity or potential threats.
4. Respond appropriately to any incidents, following established procedures for reporting and handling situations.
5. Ensure that all equipment is maintained and ready for use at all times.
6. Document all activities and observations in accordance with local SOPs.

Note: Guard duty requires strict adherence to orders and procedures to ensure the safety of personnel and assets.
guard duty, security protocols, standing operating procedures, vigilance, military operations
Perform Guard Duty According to Special Orders
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Procedure: Perform Guard Duty According to Special Orders

#### Steps:
1. Receive order changes, if any.
2. Walk posts or assume the prescribed position, carrying the weapon in the prescribed manner.
3. Salute officers when...
### Procedure: Perform Guard Duty According to Special Orders

#### Steps:
1. Receive order changes, if any.
2. Walk posts or assume the prescribed position, carrying the weapon in the prescribed manner.
3. Salute officers when performing guard duty on posts that do not require a challenge.
4. Challenge personnel as specified by special orders:
- The challenge position is port arms or raised pistol.
- Challenge one person: Assume the challenge position approximately 30 steps from the individual and state "Halt." Once the individual has halted, the guard calls "Who is there?".
- _Note_: The guard may change position to best determine if the individual should be passed, denied access or apprehended and turned over to the commander of the relief. Take the appropriate action based on identification of the person.
- Challenge a group in the same manner. If persons are in a vehicle, allow only one person to approach for identification.
- Challenge multiple groups or individuals: The guard challenges multiple groups and individuals in the same manner. The guard advances personnel to be recognized in the same manner; only one at a time and in order of seniority.
- _Note_: During a duty tour, a guard is required to execute orders ONLY from the commanding officer, the field officer of the day, the officer of the day, and officers of the guard. A guard surrenders his/her weapon only to, and only upon orders from whom he/she lawfully receives orders from while on post.
5. Pass on changes to orders to his/her relief.

#### Evaluation Preparation:
• _Setup_: At the test site, provide the Soldier with a guard post, equipment needed to operate that post, and special orders. Provide a group to approach the post.
• _Brief Soldier_: Tell the Soldier to read the special orders and assume the sentry duties of the post.

#### Performance Measures:
| Performance Measure | GO | NO GO |
|---------------------|----|-------|
| Received order changes. | —— | —— |
| Saluted officer when required (Post did not require challenging). | —— | —— |
| Challenged individual. | —— | —— |
| Challenged group. | —— | —— |
| Passed the order changes to the relief. | —— | —— |

#### Evaluation Guidance:
Refer to chapter 1, paragraph 1-9e, (1) and (2).

#### References:
• Required: Related: FM 22-6
guard duty, challenge procedure, sentry duties, special orders, urban operation, weapon handling
