Contingency Planning (1) -

Contingency Plan Development for Security Personnel
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Procedure:

1. Develop detailed contingency plans:
- Create comprehensive plans that outline the responsibilities and actions of all security personnel, including guard force and alert forces.
2. **Include specific requirements in the...
Procedure:

1. Develop detailed contingency plans:
- Create comprehensive plans that outline the responsibilities and actions of all security personnel, including guard force and alert forces.
2. Include specific requirements in the following areas:
- a. Composition and organization of the security team.
- b. Tour of duty schedules for guards.
- c. Essential posts and routes that must be protected.
- d. Weapons and equipment available to personnel.
- e. Training programs for all involved personnel.
- f. Standby procedures for military working dog teams.
- g. Method of challenging individuals using signs and countersigns.
- h. Composition, mission, weapons, location, deployment concept, and availability of alert forces.
contingency plans,security personnel,alert forces,training

Prevention Response Plans (1) -

Determine Organization Prevention and Response Procedures
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Steps:

1. Appoint a subordinate as the organization physical security officer.
2. Create a list of areas, buildings, and other structures considered critical and establish priorities for their protection. This consolidated list is the mission...
Steps:

1. Appoint a subordinate as the organization physical security officer.
2. Create a list of areas, buildings, and other structures considered critical and establish priorities for their protection. This consolidated list is the mission essential vulnerability (MEVA) list.
3. Direct the development of the organization Physical Security Plan (PSP) using FM 3-19.30, Appendix F, as a guide. Ensure that the plan reflects the commander's guidance for providing proper and economic use of personnel and equipment to prevent or minimize loss or damage from theft, espionage, sabotage, and other criminal or disruptive activities.
- Note: All physical security plans have the potential of being classified documents and must be treated accordingly.
4. Distribute the PSP to subordinate organizations to use as the basis for their organization PSP and to identify any additional METL tasks.
- Note: PSPs are reviewed and updated annually.
physical security plan,PSP development,MEVA list,security officer appointment

Resource Management (1) -

Identify Resources Necessary to Establish and Maintain the PSP
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Steps:

1. Identify resource requirements for physical security as a result of the planning process.
2. Identify requirement changes based on mission, enemy, terrain and weather, troops and support available, time available, civil considerations...
Steps:

1. Identify resource requirements for physical security as a result of the planning process.
2. Identify requirement changes based on mission, enemy, terrain and weather, troops and support available, time available, civil considerations (METT-TC).
3. Allocate resources consistent with the threat.
4. Budget for the funds necessary to provide and maintain the security program according to the timelines set forth in the Planning, Programming, Budgeting, and Execution System (PPBES).
PSP resources,METT-TC analysis,budgeting,PPBES

Document Classification (1) -

Review Document for Required Identification and Markings
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Step 1: Review information for required identification and markings.

Note: Classification markings will be in letters larger than those used in the rest of the text.

Step 2: Check the document for required markings:

• (1) Ensure...
Step 1: Review information for required identification and markings.

Note: Classification markings will be in letters larger than those used in the rest of the text.

Step 2: Check the document for required markings:

• (1) Ensure that the overall classification of the document is marked, stamped, or affixed permanently—
- (a) On the top and bottom of the outside front cover.
- (b) On the title page.
- (c) On the first page.
- (d) On the outside of the back cover.

• (2) Each interior page, except those left blank, will be marked on the top and bottom according to content, to include unclassified.

• (3) Each section, part, paragraph, or similar portion of a classified document will be marked to show the level of classification of the information contained by showing the appropriate classification symbol.

• (4) Charts, maps, and drawings will bear the appropriate classification marking for the legend, title, or scale block. The higher of these markings shall be inscribed at the top and bottom of each such document.

• (5) Photographs, films (including negatives), recordings, and their containers will be marked with the specific level of the information contained within.

• (6) Information used to simulate classified or unclassified material or documents will be marked clearly to indicate the actual unclassified status of the information (for example, SECRET for Training Purposes Only).

Step 3: Examine all classified documents for completeness; ensure that no parts or pages are missing.

Step 4: Ensure that the following appears on the face of all classified information:

• (1) The overall classification of the document should be stamped on the top and bottom.
• (2) The identity, by name or personal identifier, and position of the original classification authority.
• (3) The agency and office of origin.
• (4) Declassification instructions.
• (5) Reason for classification.
classification markings, document review, security clearance, classified information, document completeness

Intelligence Reporting (1) -

Prepare a Spot Report on Time-Sensitive Information
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Steps to Prepare a Spot Report on Time-Sensitive Information:

1. Identify Time-Sensitive Information:
- a. Refer to the technical intelligence (TECHINT) appendix to an intelligence annex for guidance on time-sensitive technical items...
Steps to Prepare a Spot Report on Time-Sensitive Information:

1. Identify Time-Sensitive Information:
- a. Refer to the technical intelligence (TECHINT) appendix to an intelligence annex for guidance on time-sensitive technical items and reporting channels.
- b. Time-sensitive information includes any significant intelligence information with either military or technical application, such as:
- Enemy order of battle
- Enemy maps, overlays, operation orders
- Signals, codes, cryptographic materiel
- New weapons or equipment on the battlefield
- Information critical to successfully accomplishing friendly courses of action
- Information that indicates a significant change in the enemy's capabilities or intentions.

2. Submit a Spot Report:
- a. Submit a spot report to notify higher commands of the capture or identification of enemy materiel believed to answer intelligence requirements.
spot report, time-sensitive information, intelligence reporting, enemy materiel, security procedures

Checkpoint Operations (1) -

Oversee Checkpoint Operations for Vehicle Traffic Control
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

(a) Ensure that convoys are checked for movement credits according to the highway traffic division (HTD). If the checkpoint is at the entrance to a controlled route, direct lost drivers to their destinations.

(b) Verify that...
Procedure:

(a) Ensure that convoys are checked for movement credits according to the highway traffic division (HTD). If the checkpoint is at the entrance to a controlled route, direct lost drivers to their destinations.

(b) Verify that vehicles are checked for movement credits according to the HTD. When checking cargo vehicles on a main supply route, cross-check manifest papers against the actual load.

(c) Remind team or squad members to be suspicious of military equipment, supplies, or weapons transported in civilian vehicles.

Evaluation Preparation:
• Schedule a field training exercise with vehicle traffic so that the Soldier can establish and supervise a roadblock, checkpoint, and holding area.
• Identify a general location for the exercise.
• Evaluate the Soldier's ability to select a specific location that offers cover and concealment.

_Brief Soldier:_
• Inform the Soldier that he/she will be evaluated as a team or squad leader.
• Emphasize that contact with the enemy is expected, and all vehicles must be stopped and checked.

Performance Measures:
1. Selected the location for the roadblock within the assigned area.
2. Established defensive positions.
3. Established communications according to the unit SOP.
4. Supervised the establishment of the roadblock.
5. Selected an appropriate checkpoint and holding area within the assigned area based on the purpose of the checkpoint.
6. Supervised the establishment of a checkpoint.
7. Supervised the establishment of the holding area.
8. Supervised the operation of a roadblock and a checkpoint.

Evaluation Guidance:
• Refer to chapter 1, paragraph 1-4 b (6) for additional guidance.

References:
• Required: FM 3-19.4
• Related: Skill Level 3, 3-174, 191-377-4203, 191-378-5315
checkpoint operations, vehicle traffic control, movement credits, roadblock supervision, installation access control point

Installation Access Control (1) -

Supervise an Installation Access Control Point
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Conditions:
• As a noncommissioned officer in charge (NCOIC), you are given an installation access control point, personnel, appropriate equipment, the current force protection condition (FPCON) threat level, and the local standing operating...
Conditions:
• As a noncommissioned officer in charge (NCOIC), you are given an installation access control point, personnel, appropriate equipment, the current force protection condition (FPCON) threat level, and the local standing operating procedure (SOP).

Standards:
• Supervise an installation access control point according to the local SOP, AR 190-13, and AR 525-13.

Procedure:
• Ensure that all personnel and vehicles entering or exiting the installation are checked in accordance with the local SOP, AR 190-13, and AR 525-13.
• Monitor compliance with force protection condition (FPCON) threat levels.
• Coordinate with other security forces to ensure seamless operations at the access control point.
• Maintain accurate records of all personnel and vehicles processed through the installation access control point.
• Ensure that all personnel are briefed on current FPCON levels and any changes in procedures.
installation access control point, force protection condition, SOP compliance, security supervision

Equipment Management (1) -

Conduct Equipment Inventory and Report Discrepancies
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Steps:

1. Conduct an equipment inventory as directed by special orders.
2. Verify that all items are accounted for and in working condition.
3. Report any discrepancies before taking responsibility for the inventory.
equipment inventory, discrepancy reporting, security management

Military Security Planning (1) -

Plan Security for a Command Post (CP)
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

Conditions: You are a military police platoon sergeant and your platoon has been given a mission to provide security for a command post (CP). You will have orders; information on the size and layout of the CP (massed or...
Procedure:

Conditions: You are a military police platoon sergeant and your platoon has been given a mission to provide security for a command post (CP). You will have orders; information on the size and layout of the CP (massed or dispersed); information on mission, enemy, terrain and weather, troops and support available, time available, civil considerations (METT-TC); and access to the provost marshal and headquarters personnel.

Standards: Develop a security plan that includes all the required elements to detect the enemy and to defend the CP before the enemy can move within direct-fire range.

Performance Steps:
1. Determine the number of personnel available to conduct CP security, to include augmented personnel, such as corps assets and the division band. Consider METT-TC (Mission, Enemy, Terrain and Weather, Troops and Support Available, Time Available, Civil Considerations) and the CP size and layout.

Notes:
• This procedure is specific to Skill Level 4.
• Ensure that all security measures are tailored to the METT-TC factors provided.
command post security plan, METT-TC, military police platoon sergeant, security planning, tactical operations

2. Define usage protocols:
- Establish guidelines for when and how the systems should be used during...

Signage (1) -

Identify Types and Posting of Warning Signs
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

1. Determine warning sign types needed:
- Select appropriate signs for different hazards (e.g., fire, restricted access, emergency exits).
2. Post signs in visible locations:
- Ensure that all signs are placed where...
Procedure:

1. Determine warning sign types needed:
- Select appropriate signs for different hazards (e.g., fire, restricted access, emergency exits).
2. Post signs in visible locations:
- Ensure that all signs are placed where they can be easily seen by personnel.
3. Maintain and update signs regularly:
- Replace or repair any damaged or outdated warning signs.
warning signs,posting locations,maintenance

Contingency Plans (1) -

Develop Contingency Plans and SOPs for Personnel Actions
Critical Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

1. Create detailed contingency plans:
- Include specific requirements such as composition, tour of duty, essential posts, weapons, training, and alert force details.
2. **Attach annexes with special orders and SOP...
Procedure:

1. Create detailed contingency plans:
- Include specific requirements such as composition, tour of duty, essential posts, weapons, training, and alert force details.
2. Attach annexes with special orders and SOP information:
- Ensure that all instructions are clearly documented and accessible to personnel.
3. Train personnel on contingency procedures:
- Conduct drills and simulations to prepare for emergencies or threats.
4. Review and update plans regularly:
- Adjust plans as needed based on new threats, equipment changes, or organizational updates.
contingency plans,SOPs,training,review

Funding Processes (1) -

Request Physical Security Funding
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Steps:

1. Request physical security requirements or enhancements beyond your budgetary means from the host commander or higher headquarters.
2. Include physical security programs and improvements as a part of all annual budgets.
3. Determine...
Steps:

1. Request physical security requirements or enhancements beyond your budgetary means from the host commander or higher headquarters.
2. Include physical security programs and improvements as a part of all annual budgets.
3. Determine which of the following three basic funding programs (subject to change) to use for the type of requirement and if sufficient funds are available:
- The RJC6, which resources physical-security equipment purchase and maintenance.
- The QLPR, which resources law-enforcement operations (to include security guards and special-reaction teams).
- The VTER, which provides resources for projects and temporary programs that enhance any type of security due to an increase in terrorist threat.
physical security funding,RJC6,QLPR,VTER,budgeting

Survival Defense (1) -

Create a Pathguard for Tactical Defense
High Priority Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Objective: Create a pathguard to provide early warning of potential threats.

1. Choose a location that is likely to be used by the enemy or other intruders (e.g., near your shelter or along travel routes).
2. Use natural materials such as...
Objective: Create a pathguard to provide early warning of potential threats.

1. Choose a location that is likely to be used by the enemy or other intruders (e.g., near your shelter or along travel routes).
2. Use natural materials such as sticks, stones, or reflective objects to create noise when disturbed.
3. Ensure that the pathguard is concealed from view but still effective in alerting you of movement.
4. Position the pathguard at an appropriate tactical distance from your shelter (e.g., 10–20 feet away).
5. Regularly check and maintain the pathguard to ensure it remains functional.
pathguard, tactical defense, early warning system

Door Locks (1) -

Constructing a Compass Lock for a Door
Standard Source: Shelters Shacks and Shanties
### Materials Needed:
• Three old door-knobs
• A block of hardwood
• Screws and nuts
• A square block (for shaping the wooden block)
• A metal washer or circular tin disk
• Nails or screws to fasten disks on the door
• A steel shaft from a...
### Materials Needed:
• Three old door-knobs
• A block of hardwood
• Screws and nuts
• A square block (for shaping the wooden block)
• A metal washer or circular tin disk
• Nails or screws to fasten disks on the door
• A steel shaft from a door-knob

### Steps:
1. Prepare the Wooden Block:
- Start with a square block of hardwood.
- Cut off the corners as shown by the dotted lines in the text, then whittle the angles until it becomes rounded like a compass face.
- Saw off an arc (part of a circle) to create a shape similar to a compass face (referenced in Figs. 224, 226, and 227).

2. Create a Square Hole:
- Make a square hole through the center of the wooden block. This hole must be centered on the original circular shape of the block, not its current rounded form.

3. Attach to Steel Shaft:
- Insert the square end of the steel shaft into the square hole in the wooden block.
- Carefully drill a hole through the end of the steel shaft and insert a screw through it to secure the wooden block permanently on the end of the knob.

4. Install Door-Knobs:
- Take three door-knobs (as shown in Figs. 222, 224, and 225) and attach them to the door before securing the blocks permanently.

5. Mark Compass Directions:
- Use a metal washer or circular tin disk and divide it into compass points as shown in Fig. 228.
- Fasten these disks securely on the door with nails or screws, ensuring that all disks have their north point pointing to the top of the door and are aligned with each other.

6. File Notches:
- File a small notch into the circular base of each door-knob at the position where the index mark is located (as shown in Fig. 224).

7. Set Combination:
- Turn the knobs so that their flat edges are parallel with the crack of the door, allowing it to open freely.
- Record the positions of the index marks on each knob (e.g., northeast, southwest, south) in a notebook for future reference.

8. Locking Mechanism:
- When closing the door, twist the knobs so that their blocks overlap the crack of the door, as shown in Fig. 227. This will lock the door and prevent it from being opened without knowing the combination.

### Notes:
• The combination is determined by aligning the index marks on each knob to match the recorded positions.
• The use of compass points instead of numbers makes the combination less obvious to outsiders.
• These locks are designed to keep out tramps, vagrants, and inquisitive boys, not professional burglars.
compass lock, door lock, homemade lock, security, DIY lock

• Stay informed through trusted sources such as the national broadcaster (ERR).

• **Avoid sharing false or inflammatory information that could endanger...
- Follow all instructions provided by authorities if you are in a conflict zone.

• Stay informed through trusted sources such as the national broadcaster (ERR).

• Avoid sharing false or inflammatory information that could endanger individuals involved in conflicts.

• Take breaks from consuming information to avoid overload and maintain mental well-being.

Facility Protection (1) -

Protecting Emergency Response Operations Centers
Critical Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Identify and secure all critical infrastructure within emergency response operations centers.
2. Implement physical security measures such as access controls, surveillance systems, and perimeter fencing to prevent unauthorized...
Procedure:

1. Identify and secure all critical infrastructure within emergency response operations centers.
2. Implement physical security measures such as access controls, surveillance systems, and perimeter fencing to prevent unauthorized entry.
3. Ensure that backup power supplies are available and regularly tested to maintain functionality during outages.
4. Establish communication protocols with local law enforcement for rapid response in case of threats or breaches.
5. Conduct regular drills and simulations to prepare staff for potential security incidents or disruptions.
emergency operations center,security measures,backup power,communication protocols,drills

Law Enforcement Operations (1) -

Managing Surge in Crime During Pandemic
High Priority Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Analyze crime data to identify trends and hotspots that may increase during a pandemic.
2. Increase police presence in high-risk areas while ensuring officer safety through proper PPE and equipment.
3. Collaborate with community...
Procedure:

1. Analyze crime data to identify trends and hotspots that may increase during a pandemic.
2. Increase police presence in high-risk areas while ensuring officer safety through proper PPE and equipment.
3. Collaborate with community organizations to address underlying issues contributing to increased criminal activity.
4. Implement rapid response protocols for emergencies such as domestic violence, theft, or public disturbances.
5. Use technology tools like surveillance cameras and data analytics to monitor crime patterns in real-time.

Correctional Facility Operations (1) -

Implementing Isolation Measures in Correctional Facilities
High Priority Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Identify individuals within correctional facilities who may be at higher risk of infection or who are symptomatic.
2. Implement strict isolation protocols for these individuals, ensuring they receive adequate medical care and...
Procedure:

1. Identify individuals within correctional facilities who may be at higher risk of infection or who are symptomatic.
2. Implement strict isolation protocols for these individuals, ensuring they receive adequate medical care and monitoring.
3. Provide PPE to staff working with isolated inmates and ensure that all areas used by infected individuals are properly disinfected.
4. Limit visitation and movement within the facility to reduce the risk of disease transmission.
5. Train correctional officers on infection control practices and emergency response procedures specific to isolation scenarios.

Fraud Prevention (1) -

Protect Yourself from Disaster-Related Fraud and Scams
High Priority Source: FEMA Citizen Preparedness Guide

1. Be cautious of anyone asking for personal information or money in exchange for assistance.
2. If you are unsure about the legitimacy of a request, do not provide any personal details.
3. Report...
Steps to Avoid Disaster-Related Fraud:

1. Be cautious of anyone asking for personal information or money in exchange for assistance.
2. If you are unsure about the legitimacy of a request, do not provide any personal details.
3. Report suspicious individuals claiming to be government workers to local authorities immediately.
4. Be wary of scams that promise quick solutions or financial aid in exchange for upfront payments.
5. Always verify the identity and credentials of anyone offering assistance after a disaster.

Recognize Signs of a Chemical Release

Procedure:

1. Be aware of the following signs that may indicate a chemical release:
- People experiencing difficulty breathing.
- Eye irritation or burning sensations in the nose, throat, and lungs.
- Nausea or loss of...
Procedure:

1. Be aware of the following signs that may indicate a chemical release:
- People experiencing difficulty breathing.
- Eye irritation or burning sensations in the nose, throat, and lungs.
- Nausea or loss of coordination.
- The presence of many dead insects or birds.
2. Report any suspicious activity to local authorities immediately.

Chemical Biological Attack (1) -

Safest Room During a Chemical or Biological Attack at Home
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Procedure:

1. Choose an interior room with no windows to minimize exposure to chemical agents.
2. Ensure the room has a door that can be sealed tightly, if possible, using plastic sheeting and duct tape for added protection.
3. **Avoid...
Procedure:

1. Choose an interior room with no windows to minimize exposure to chemical agents.
2. Ensure the room has a door that can be sealed tightly, if possible, using plastic sheeting and duct tape for added protection.
3. Avoid rooms near utility lines or vents where hazardous substances might enter.
4. Stock the room with essential supplies such as water, food, medications, and emergency communication devices.
5. Ensure all occupants know how to seal the room quickly in case of an attack.
safe room,chemical attack,biological attack,home,preparation

Threat Detection (1) -

Report Suspicious Activities Near Levees
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Identify suspicious activities that may indicate surveillance or potential threats to levees, including:
- Presence of strangers or unknown vehicles near the levee over multiple days.
- Individuals in public areas who do...
Procedure:

1. Identify suspicious activities that may indicate surveillance or potential threats to levees, including:
- Presence of strangers or unknown vehicles near the levee over multiple days.
- Individuals in public areas who do not fit typical profiles (e.g., walkers, joggers, boaters).
- Use of cameras, video devices, or optical equipment for photographing or diagramming levee structures or support systems.
- Aircraft or UAS flyovers in restricted airspace; boat encroachment into restricted areas; unusual recovery or tossing of items.
- Unusual maintenance activities (e.g., road repairs) near the asset.
- Cuts in fencing or gates to key areas without resulting theft, vandalism, or petty criminal activity.
- Unusual requests for information from individuals approaching levee employees, friends, or family members; or calling, visiting, or sending emails with questions about levee operations.
- Theft of marked vehicles, work clothes, or identification cards from levee personnel, contractors, or other authorized personnel.
- Unauthorized access attempts to computers, systems, or websites for personal information, maps, or targeting examples.

2. Report suspicious activities immediately to local law enforcement, the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), or State and local Fusion Centers.

3. Provide detailed information when reporting, including:
- A description of the suspicious behavior.
- Details about individuals, vehicles, or other relevant entities involved.
- Any photographic evidence that does not compromise personnel safety.

4. Use the Homeland Security Information Network-Critical Sectors (HSIN-CI) Dams Portal for real-time awareness and information sharing regarding potential suspicious activities at levees and dams. This tool is available through the U.S. Department of Homeland Security and allows levee owners and operators to evaluate reports from a broad range of agencies and organizations.

5. Contact the Dams Sector-Specific Agency within the U.S. Department of Homeland Security for access to the HSIN-CI Dams Portal at *dams@hq.dhs.gov*.

6. Use good judgment and experience when identifying suspicious activities, as some items used in surveillance efforts may also be commonly used by recreational individuals (e.g., campers, hikers, photographers).
suspicious activity,reporting,surveillance,levee security,fbi,fusion centers,hsin-ci

System Security (1) -

Define Logical Security Requirements for Alert Systems
Standard Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Logical Security – Description of logical security (cyber-security) requirements, such as prevention of unauthorized or malicious access to the system, or accidental or malicious actions resulting in denial of service and other cyber-security.
logical security,cybersecurity,alert systems

Civil Unrest (1) -

Dealing with Riots and Civil Unrest
Critical Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Stay Informed: Monitor local news, social media, and official alerts for updates on riots or civil unrest.
2. Avoid Affected Areas: If a riot is occurring in your neighborhood or city, avoid the area unless absolutely...
Procedure:

1. Stay Informed: Monitor local news, social media, and official alerts for updates on riots or civil unrest.
2. Avoid Affected Areas: If a riot is occurring in your neighborhood or city, avoid the area unless absolutely necessary.
3. Secure Your Home: Lock doors and windows, and if possible, reinforce them with barricades or additional security measures.
4. Stock Emergency Supplies: Keep a supply of water, food, batteries, flashlights, and first-aid supplies in case of prolonged disruptions.
5. Stay Calm and Avoid Confrontation: If you are caught in a riot, avoid engaging with participants or law enforcement unless it is absolutely necessary for your safety.
6. Follow Official Instructions: Listen to instructions from local authorities or emergency management officials regarding evacuation routes or shelter-in-place orders.
7. Document Events: If safe to do so, take photos or videos of the situation as evidence for future legal or insurance purposes.
8. Support Community Recovery Efforts: After a riot subsides, assist in cleanup and restoration efforts if possible.
riot,civil unrest,safety,emergency preparedness,evacuation

Lockdown Procedure (1) -

Implement Lockdown Procedure for Childcare Facility
Critical Source: GSAChildCareEmergencyPreparednessToolbox June 2014
Steps to follow upon hearing the chosen lockdown announcement:

1. Check immediate areas: Staff should quickly check the hall and restrooms closest to their classrooms to get children into the classrooms.
2. Secure the room:
- Lock...
Steps to follow upon hearing the chosen lockdown announcement:

1. Check immediate areas: Staff should quickly check the hall and restrooms closest to their classrooms to get children into the classrooms.
2. Secure the room:
- Lock all doors (ensure they lock properly).
- Close and lock all windows.
- Cover all windows and doors with available materials.
- Turn off lights.
3. Position children safely: Keep children away from windows and doors; position them in a safe place against walls or on the floor.
4. Create barriers: Turn a classroom table on its side to use as a buffer for added protection.
5. Maintain calm atmosphere: Staff should remain calm, keeping alert to the emotional needs of the children.
6. Gather children in a safe area:
- Gather in a story circle behind the table.
- Place infants into one or two cribs (preferably on wheels) along with items to help keep them quiet, such as bottles, pacifiers, and small, quiet toys.
7. Keep children in classroom: Teachers will keep all children in the classroom until an “all clear” signal has been given.
8. Call 911 immediately:
- The director or designee should call 911 and stay on the phone until help arrives.
- Await further instructions from emergency response personnel.
- Do not release children to parents until an “all clear” has been called.
9. Follow police protocol: Upon arrival, local police will assume control and may evacuate the building per their standard operating procedure.
10. Resume normal activities after all clear:
- When “All Clear” is heard, the director will apprise staff of the situation and counsel with children.
- Resume normal activities as soon as possible, as instructed by the director.
11. Inform parents: The director will inform parents of all lockdowns, whether practice or real.
12. Report incident:
- The director will report the incident to the licensor.
- Complete a written incident report at the earliest opportunity and submit it to the Property Management Office.
lockdown procedure, childcare emergency, secure room, call 911, all clear signal

Staff Safety (1) -

Establish a Safety and Security Office for Staff Protection
High Priority Source: Home Health Emergency Preparedness
Procedure:

1. Create a dedicated safety and security office within the agency to identify potential risks and implement mitigation strategies.
2. Avoid deploying staff just before the onset of serious weather events or other...
Procedure:

1. Create a dedicated safety and security office within the agency to identify potential risks and implement mitigation strategies.
2. Avoid deploying staff just before the onset of serious weather events or other emergencies.
3. Develop policies and procedures that address:
- Working with local authorities to ensure staff have proper identification and permissions for travel during road closures.
- Providing safe transport for staff when conditions warrant it.
- Facilitating rapid decontamination protocols for staff after exposure to hazardous materials or environments.
- Planning for the safe evacuation of office premises in case of disaster.
- Locating all staff quickly and efficiently when a disaster is declared.
- Ensuring that appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) is available and accessible to all staff.
- Distributing winter travel kits with supplies such as ice traction devices, emergency blankets, and warm clothing for icy road conditions.
4. Educate staff on the limits of their roles during disasters, emphasizing not to put themselves in danger while providing services.
safety, security office, staff protection, disaster protocols, PPE, winter travel kits

Emergency Security (1) -

Security Officer's Role During Emergency
Critical Source: Home Health Emergency Preparedness
Procedure:

1. The Security Officer’s role is to ensure the safety of staff and patients and support the security of agency operations.

2. The Security Officer will make assessments regarding the security of the command center, the safety and...
Procedure:

1. The Security Officer’s role is to ensure the safety of staff and patients and support the security of agency operations.

2. The Security Officer will make assessments regarding the security of the command center, the safety and travel conditions for staff and make arrangements for relocation of the command center, transportation and/or safety escorts as needed.

3. The Security Officer will also ensure all staff has needed identifying badges and/or uniforms which will allow them access to their agency.

4. The Security Officer will work with agency leadership and local/county officials to ensure safe passage and access to patient homes when roads are closed.
security officer, emergency security, command center relocation, safety escorts

2. Consider implementing **visual...
Objective: Enable rapid identification of staff and emergency workers during disasters.

Steps:

2. Consider implementing visual identifiers, such as badges, uniforms, or color-coded tags for easy recognition.
3. Ensure all staff are trained on this identification system during drills and exercises.
staff identification, emergency worker recognition, disaster plan

Decontamination (1) -

Implement Pre-Hospital Triage and Decontamination Procedures
High Priority Source: Home Health Emergency Preparedness
Objective: Safely manage potentially contaminated victims before hospital entry.

Steps:
1. Develop a pre-hospital triage/decontamination plan for disaster scenarios involving biological, chemical, or nuclear contaminants.
2. Establish...
Objective: Safely manage potentially contaminated victims before hospital entry.

Steps:
1. Develop a pre-hospital triage/decontamination plan for disaster scenarios involving biological, chemical, or nuclear contaminants.
2. Establish designated areas for decontamination and ensure they are equipped with:
- Appropriate PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)
- Decontamination supplies
3. Train staff on triage protocols, including:
- Identifying symptoms of exposure
- Proper handling and transport of contaminated individuals
4. Ensure the plan is tested during drills and updated regularly.
